items.q: What are the 4 Cs of food hygiene?
items.correct: Cleaning, Cooking, Chilling, and avoiding Cross-contamination.
items.why: Students guess at the Cs. They specifically address the four food-safety risks: dirty surfaces, undercooked food, warm storage, and pathogens transferring between foods (raw to ready-to-eat).

items.q: On a traffic-light label, what does a red light indicate?
items.correct: High amounts of that nutrient per 100g - eat less often.
items.misconception: The food is dangerous and should never be eaten.
items.why: Students treat red as 'forbidden'. Traffic lights are a guidance system - red = high, eat less often or in small portions. Many foods (cheese, olive oil) are red on certain nutrients but fine in moderation.

items.q: Which vitamin is most likely to be deficient in a poorly planned vegan diet?
items.why: Students guess vitamin C (it's the famous one), but plant foods are rich in C. B12 is found almost exclusively in animal products, so vegans need fortified foods or supplements.

items.q: Are the Maillard reaction and caramelisation the same process?
items.correct: No - the Maillard reaction is between proteins and sugars (e.g. searing meat); caramelisation is sugar alone breaking down with heat.
items.misconception: Yes - they are just two names for sugar browning.
items.random_wrong: Caramelisation only happens in savoury food.
items.why: Both produce browning, so students merge them. Maillard browning needs protein AND sugar under dry heat (meat crusts, bread crusts, toast). Caramelisation needs only sugar at high temperature (caramel, browned onions' sweetness). Different chemistry, different flavours.

items.q: What is the relationship between denaturation and coagulation of proteins?
items.correct: Denaturation (unfolding of the protein) happens first; coagulation is when the unfolded proteins then bond together and set.
items.misconception: Denaturation and coagulation are the same single change.
items.why: Students use the words interchangeably. They are two stages: heat, acid or whisking first unravels the protein's structure (denaturation); the unfolded chains can then link to each other and set firm (coagulation), as when a runny egg becomes solid.

items.q: How many portions of fruit and veg a day are recommended?
items.why: Correct answer: 5. “10” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Eatwell Guide is from which country?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: UK. “USA” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Recommended portions of oily fish per week?
items.why: The real figure is 1 portion. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
